title: Today's Tech Brief: Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch
summary: A brief look at tech headlines, including Norse Atlantic Airways' ticket refunds and climate tech going public.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch
imageCaption: Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch
imageSourceTitle: Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I've got a few tech stories to share with you today. First up, Norse Atlantic Airways has been making waves with their dirt-cheap tickets, but there's a catch. Apparently, some customers have been receiving emails saying their flights were canceled, and they had just 14 days to request a refund. Now, I'm no expert, but that sounds like a pretty tight deadline to me. And, as it turns out, some of these emails might be scams. According to a report from Wired, AI-generated spam is on the rise, and companies like Norse Atlantic Airways need to stay on top of it to protect their customers.

In other news, climate tech companies are going public, and it's exciting to see solar and battery companies leading the charge. This could mean more investment and innovation in the space, which is great news for the planet. And, on a related note, the AI Hype Index is back, which is a fun way to track the ups and downs of AI enthusiasm. I'm not sure if it's a perfect measure, but it's definitely a interesting one.

Norse Atlantic Airways Offers Dirt-Cheap Tickets. There’s a Catch is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: On March 31, I received an email from Norse Atlantic Airways. The $940 flights for my upcoming round trip to Rome had been canceled, it said, and I had 14 days to request a refund. At first, I didn’t panic. That began to change when the company’s refund request page wouldn’t load on two browsers across three devices. After Norse didn’t respond to several em... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

The Download: climate tech goes public and the AI Hype Index returns is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: This is today's edition of The Download , our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what's going on in the world of technology. Climate tech companies are going public. What’s next? Solar and battery company Solv Energy went public in February, hitting a $6 billion valuation. X-energy, which builds small modular nuclear reactors, followed at $11.... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
